sentences.text: My name is Eddie Ilan.
Eignatz Brzezowski.
B-R-Z-E-Z-O-W-S-K-I.
Sochaczew.
It's-- S.
If I can remember.
S-C-H-O-C-Z-E-W.
Poland.
I-- I think I was born on 7/14/1928.
My sister says that I was born in '29.
So I don't remember.
I consider myself 67.
We lived in this little town.
I know now how far it is from Warsaw.
At that time I didn't know.
It's 53 kilometers west of Warsaw.
And not too many Jews lived there.
I'd say a couple of thousand.
Because I don't remember at that time how many were there.
I went to school, I went to cheder, and I went to yavneh.
And I also went, later on to [POLISH], which is the regular school.
There were two Jews in the school, me and somebody else.
And everyday life--
Well, there was-- yes, there was.
We-- right across from where we lived there was like a shopping center.

And there was, every Friday when the farmers in the villages came to this little village to shop for the weekend, there was a sign out there that was scribbled on, [NON-ENGLISH] which means, if you buy from a Jew, you're a dog.
And that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? paper, ?] as I remember.
I got beat up many a times.
I got beat up many of times just-- just because, when I went to [POLISH], they would call me Zyd.
And that was an accepted part-- an accepted part of life.
Well for one thing, the teachers didn't hide it, the school didn't hide it, and I didn't hide it.
In order to belong someplace and to defend myself, so to speak, I even joined [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was like a family you belonged to.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The-- what should I tell you?
I remember my parents.
My father was very orthodox.
He was a shoemaker.
Where did he have his shop?
No shop.
We lived in-- in this little town on [?
Varshavska ?] Street, number 15.
I don't remember.
My sister told me, and I believe her.
I do remember the room that we lived in.
There were five of us, one room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I was the only poor one in Europe.
I don't know.
But we were poor.
And my mother used to-- used to cook for the farmers-- the villagers-- that came to the city on Friday-- on Tuesday and Friday.
And I would take off from school and go and carry the food out to them to make a zloty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once a month, on the first of the month, there was like a big market.
It came from far-- further away.
And I would take a bucket of soda pops, called [? kvass, ?] and go around between the horses and the cows as they were selling them to give to the people and make a half of dollar-- a zloty, rather.
So [? kvass ?] was like a cool drink.
A soda pop.
A soda pop.
And that I would do once a month.
That I do remember.
And I remember one policeman.
His name was Albert.
That I do remember.
And the only thing he knew was how to beat up Jewish kids.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
When I went back this time, in 1993, and asked the curator of the museum if he knew about Albert, he's the only one that remembered him.
And he said, oh, Albert the cow?
He says, I remember him.
I said, do you remember how he used to beat up the Jewish kids?
He says, I don't remember.
And this man should remember.
He was older than I am.
He was in his late 80s in 1993, so he should remember.
But I guess, like all of them, they don't.
Or they don't want to remember.
I had three sisters.
And most of them-- well, all three of them-- are bigger than I am.
My oldest sister is now living in Israel.
My middle sister was killed in Poland for a bottle of vodka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was an edict in Poland at that time after the War to-- for a liter of vodka, kill a Jew or bring him in alive.
Well, she was killed.
And my small sister, my little sister, she was in the Warsaw ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And now I don't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's about all I can tell you about this little town.
I was too small even to-- I was just--
Well, according to--
According to me, I was 10 years old when the war broke out.
According to my sister, I was nine.
So I don't-- it's very hard.
It's-- when I went back now, everything was so big, huge.
The town hasn't changed a bit.
But it was big, because at that-- at that time.
Now in two steps I cross the street.
It's a big difference.
Well, the only thing I can remember, actually, is going to shul, going to cheder.
We lived in a four corner building.
In one of the rooms was, like, a prayer house called a shtibl.
But the shul was, at that time, a long ways away.
But actually, it wasn't.
It was only four blocks away.
But you didn't go there very often.
You went to the shtibl.
Just on holidays we went there.
Yeah.
I don't remember.
Honest, I don't.
We went to out eat.
See, I don't remember if I ever had.
The only thing I remember is my mother saying, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's better to have a patch on top of a patch [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then to have a torn spot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That I remember.
[CRYING] I'm sorry.
Yes, I guess so.
With what she had.
I guess so.
I remember Passover we would get some matzohs from-- from America.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember on Friday she would buy the little fishes that were maybe two inches long, and skin them and chop them for hours until they were nice and chopped up so we could have fish for Friday night.
Yeah.
Out of those little fish.
Gefilte?
Gefilte fish?
No, the actual fish.
Yeah.
No, I don't remember.
They were not bigger than a finger.
My sisters went to school, so I don't-- I really don't remember.
All I know is that Friday night we had gefilte fish.
And I remember there was one gentile, a farmer, who came to town about once a month.
And he, like of, adopted me.
He wanted me to be his god-- his godson.
Because when I was born, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] according to my parents, according to him, he came in and bought a pair of shoes.
At the moment of your birth?
That I remember.
They told me.
So I remembered him coming.
And I had to take off from school, be in the house, and as far as I can remember, I had to drink a shot of vodka with him.
That was expected and--
I just thought that was a nice man who deserved to be nice to.
But as a kid, I didn't pay any attention to all of that.
All I know is he came in, I jumped on his knee, and he gave me five groszy to go and buy a ice cream.
That was-- it was called a lody.
Lody.
Yes.
Lody.
Lody.
Yeah.
And after I had my drink of vodka, I went out and played and that was it.
I guess so.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I wish I would.
Yeah.
We had, in this four square building, there were mostly Jews living there.
The friends that I had were mainly from school.
And we played soccer.
And we were too young to go to the cinema.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had two of them in this little town.
But we would hang out in the little market.
We thought it was big, and now it's small.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my sisters had friends.
They used to come into the house.
But that's about all I can remember.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the smallest, and I was a boy.
So I was considered the Kaddish.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They tell me-- told me-- my sister still is telling me-- that I was treated the best of them all because--
Because I was the Kaddish.
That's-- when they die, I would--
I thought I-- I thought I-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
I don't remember.
I must have.
I must have.
I was taken to shul.
My sisters were not.
My father.
I liked it because there were kids there.
We played, we jumped around.
I remember every one of us had payot.
Yeah.
Yeah, I--
So that kind of marked you to be very-- as a Jew.
Right.
Right.
Just regular clothes.
Well, my father used to make a pair of shoes that had to last four years.
So the first year they were real big and then we put some cotton into them so I can fit in.
And then every year, he would take out the little cotton.
So by the time the shoes were worn out, my foot fit in it perfect.
That was a way of doing it, I guess.
I think so.
People came back.
No.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a way of life, and I didn't think I was going anyplace, do anything.
Because I didn't think.
It was not a part of to think.
It was just to go to school, come home, eat, go out and play.
That's it.
I guess I must have.
I didn't run away.
I must have been happy.
My family was happy.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We lived in-- I could have-- couldn't have lived there without seeing everything.
We lived in a one room, five people.
So I had to see it.
[INAUDIBLE]
You saw a lot of your parents and their life together.
Yeah.
Until today, I can't figure out how five people can live in one room and have three kids.
I don't-- I can't understand it.
But I guess when you live in that kind of life, everything comes out fine.
There was a little place on the side as you walked into the door.
There was a stove for wood.
We had-- we had no refrigerator, so-- but there was a piece of the door that was cut out.
A piece of wood like a hole.
So we can put the food there so we can keep it cool, because Poland is cold all the time.
And we had two beds, and at night, the table was moved and another cot was-- a bed was opened up for the two-- for the two big sisters.
I slept with my father, and my little sister slept with my mother.
And my other two sisters slept together.
In another one.
I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before winter, the-- the farmer would come and unload potatoes and we would put them under the bed.
We had no place else, I guess.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember, Friday we would take a pot of potatoes, [? cold chilled, ?] and take it to the bakery, which was in the same four buildings.
And Saturday after shul we would go and take it.
And we would eat it for lunch, and then put the pot under the [NON-ENGLISH], under the blankets to keep it warm so we can have it later, because you could not have a fire.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember there was a big party in the building when we got lights.
Electricity.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had [? lafta. ?] That I remember.
Lafta. ?] Lamps.
Yeah.
And then we got one bulb in.
It was like a God sent light.
No, I don't remember.
But it was before the war, because I remember getting it.
I remember.
But I don't remember which year.
Years to me at that time was nothing.
I was--
You were a little boy.
Let's go out and play soccer.
Yeah, sure.
Where we lived, all Jewish boys.
Yeah.
We could not, or would not, go and play with the other boys because they would just beat the things out of you and-- so we just decided not to go and play with them.
They didn't come here.
We didn't go there.
Many a times.
Nothing.
Well, that's why--
Well, that's why I joined [NON-ENGLISH] so that when-- I don't remember how long it was, how it was before the war.
I mean, how many years.
But I must have been a baby.
Yeah, a little boy.
Yeah.
When-- when we went out, there were a bunch of us who would never go alone.
Like, even to walk down the street from one corner to the other, we would go as a bunch.
You had to.
You had to.
But you couldn't fight because, if you fought, the cops would come and take you away and-- and beat you up, this Albert.
We had only--
The Jewish kids they would take away and beat them up?
Yeah.
There was only one policeman and he was the worst there ever was.
So we learned, but we couldn't.
We couldn't fight back.
So--
Sure.
Sure.
To whom?
What they said when I told them I was beaten up?
They never said fight back.
They just said, next time, run.
Run.
Don't get into a fight.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that was not only me.
It was a way of life.
You know you're going to go out, you're going to go for a walk, you're going to get hit, you're going to get kicked, you're going to be spat on, and you're going to be called Zyd.
And I guess maybe it was an identification that I was a Zyd.
But I believed that I was a Zyd.
I am a Zyd.
So that was part of life I guess.
At that time?
No.
We wouldn't talk about it.
My parents wouldn't talk about it.
No, they would say, we are Jewish, we are Orthodox.
We are religious.
We believe in God.
And when you go to cheder, they teach you things.
But it was-- my parents, I guess they-- now I know they never philosophised.
They never said anything.
They had to read.
They-- they were there reading in Hebrew, reading the Bible.
My father-- I don't know if he went to [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
I don't know if my mother did, but I know my sisters did.
My mother had a fixation I guess-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you must learn.
You must learn.
She put importance on that?
Yes.
No matter what, she says, you must learn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If it's Jewish, or Hebrew, or Polish-- learn.
I don't know.
Did I learn?
What did I learn?
Do I read Polish?
Yes.
I read Polish.
I wrote Polish, Yiddish, Hebrew.
I could [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] say the morning prayers by heart.
In Hebrew.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I could say-- I could speak a little Hebrew.
I could say evening prayers almost by heart, I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke Polish pretty good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still do.
But I don't speak it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think I should.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should not categorize it as all of Poland, but most of the Polish people don't deserve to have me speak their language.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's my feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Did I learn some from the Polish?
Yes I did.
I learned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that if you hate, you're in trouble.
You're very nasty.
So I learned not to hate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But up to a point, I can't forgive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I guess I'll die with it, that I can't forgive certain things that shouldn't have been done to me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't do anything to them.
I was a little baby.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How bad could I have been at 9, 10 years old?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I guess that's life.
Do you remember what happened when war broke out?
Yeah.
I remember.
